title: Evolution of Non-Stationary Quasi-Periodic Pulsations in Solar Flares with Aditya-L1/HEL1OS and GOES
comment: 18 pages, 7 figures, Accepted for publication in Proceedings of the Royal Society A
summary: In the present work, we use a phase-resolved methodology for the detection and characterisation of non-stationary QPPs in the hard X-ray lightcurves observed from the HEL1OS instrument onboard Aditya-L1 mission, complemented by cross-examination using soft X-ray observations from GOES. Our approach combines variational mode decomposition with Hilbert-Huang analysis and wavelet transforms to sharpen transient QPP signatures, extract narrowband amplitude-frequency modulated modes, and track instantaneous period drift across various phases of the flares. By applying these tools to preflare, impulsive, and decay phases and associated intervals, we identify the timing, duration, and evolution of statistically significant oscillations and assess their relation to thermal and non-thermal flare components. Finally, we extend the method to the catalogue of non-stationary QPP flares reported by Mehta et al. (2023), where we identify statistically significant QPPs in both impulsive and decay phases for 74 out of 98 flares. Among these, 56.8% exhibit small period drifts, while 43.2% show larger deviations. Positive period drift dominates (73%), indicating a systematic increase in characteristic timescales as flares evolve. A phase-period-duration analysis reveals distinct regimes of non-stationarity, including overlapping multi-mode oscillations, intermittent same-period episodes, and strongly evolving multi-modal behaviour.

AI.tldr: A phase-resolved methodology combining variational mode decomposition, Hilbert-Huang analysis, and wavelet transforms is used to detect and characterize non-stationary quasi-periodic pulsations (QPPs) in hard X-ray lightcurves from the HEL1OS instrument on Aditya-L1, cross-validated with GOES soft X-ray data. Applied to 98 flares, 74 show significant QPPs, with 73% exhibiting positive period drift (increasing timescales) and distinct non-stationarity regimes identified.
AI.motivation: To detect and characterize non-stationary quasi-periodic pulsations (QPPs) in solar flares using phase-resolved analysis across different flare phases, leveraging the new HEL1OS/Aditya-L1 hard X-ray observations combined with GOES soft X-ray data.
AI.method: Phase-resolved methodology combining variational mode decomposition (VMD), Hilbert-Huang transform (HHT), and wavelet analysis applied to preflare, impulsive, and decay phases; cross-examination with GOES soft X-ray data.
AI.result: 74 out of 98 flares show significant QPPs; 56.8% have small period drifts, 43.2% larger deviations; 73% show positive period drift (systematic increase in timescales); distinct non-stationarity regimes identified (overlapping multi-mode, intermittent same-period, strongly evolving multi-modal).
AI.conclusion: The combined phase-resolved approach effectively characterizes non-stationary QPPs across flare phases, revealing systematic period evolution patterns and distinct non-stationarity regimes in solar flare QPPs.

title: Hints of enhanced magnetic activity after the intermediate rotation period gap as traced by the chromospheric Ca ii infrared triplet
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A. 15 pages, 9 figures, 3 tables. Machine-readable tables are available at https://zenodo.org/records/21086333
summary: For low-mass stars (M < 1.4 Msun), the connection between stellar rotation and magnetic activity governs stellar spin-down, shapes the environments of their exoplanets, and provides an age-diagnostic via magneto-gyro-chronology. Recently, unexpected phenomena known as the intermediate rotation period gap and the rotational stalling have been discovered. These are likely due to internal angular momentum redistribution, and mark departures from a smooth spin-down evolution. These features have been shown to cause enhanced magnetic activity on the photosphere, as measured by the photometric index from light curves (Sph), in both cluster and field stars. However, their influence on other magnetic activity proxies, and particularly in field stars, remains poorly understood. In this work, we study the impact of the intermediate-period gap on chromospheric magnetic activity as traced by the Ca ii infrared triplet (IRT) index. We target the stars observed by the Kepler mission, as this is the largest and most reliable sample of field stars with measured rotation periods sensitive to the gap. We calculate the Ca ii IRT index for the Kepler stars using the spectroscopic information from the Gaia mission data release three (DR3). We study the rotation-activity relation as a function of spectral type, finding that K dwarfs are more active than G dwarfs, which in turn are more active than F dwarfs. For main-sequence stars, we find that chromospheric magnetic activity is also enhanced after the intermediate-period gap, mirroring its effect on the photospheric Sph index. Our work reveals that the intermediate-period gap marks a genuine transition in stellar magnetic behavior, not only at the photosphere but also at the chromosphere. This highlights the need to account for its signatures across activity proxies, as well as its impact on exoplanet habitability and the age-rotation-activity relation.

AI.tldr: This study examines how the intermediate rotation period gap (a feature in stellar spin-down evolution) affects chromospheric magnetic activity in low-mass Kepler field stars using Ca ii infrared triplet indices from Gaia DR3. The authors find that chromospheric activity is enhanced after the gap, similar to previously observed photospheric effects, indicating a genuine transition in magnetic behavior across stellar atmospheres.
AI.motivation: The intermediate rotation period gap and rotational stalling are newly discovered phenomena in low-mass stellar spin-down that affect photospheric magnetic activity. However, their impact on other magnetic activity proxies (especially chromospheric indicators) and in field stars remains poorly understood, motivating this investigation.
AI.method: The authors analyze Kepler field stars (the largest reliable sample with rotation periods sensitive to the gap), compute Ca ii infrared triplet (IRT) indices using Gaia DR3 spectroscopy, and study the rotation-activity relation as a function of spectral type (F, G, K dwarfs).
AI.result: K dwarfs are more chromospherically active than G dwarfs, which are more active than F dwarfs. Chromospheric magnetic activity (Ca ii IRT) is enhanced after the intermediate-period gap for main-sequence stars, mirroring the behavior previously observed for the photospheric Sph index.
AI.conclusion: The intermediate-period gap marks a genuine transition in stellar magnetic behavior affecting both photosphere and chromosphere. This underscores the need to account for its signatures across activity proxies and its implications for exoplanet habitability and age-rotation-activity relations.

title: TESS light curves and surface activity in two low-mass eclipsing binaries: NSVS 01031772 and 2MASS J04100497+2931023
comment: 17 pages, 11 figures, 11 tables, accepted for publication in New Astronomy
summary: The VRIC light curves were regularly measured for two eclipsing binaries, NSVS 01031772 and 2MASS J04100497+2931023 as part of our long-term observational project to study low-mass eclipsing binaries with a short orbital period and surface activity. The solution of the TESS light curves in PHOEBE results in a detached configuration. Absolute parameters of all components were improved: for N103: M1 = 0.5475 +/- 0.0035 M\sun, R1 = 0.5297 +/- 0.0035 R\sun, M2 = 0.5038 +/- 0.0040 M\sun, R2 = 0.5217 +/- 0.0035 R\sun, for 2M0410: M1 = 0.639 +/- 0.045 M\sun, R1 = 0.655 +/- 0.035 R\sun, M2 = 0.609 +/- 0.045 M\sun, R2 = 0.631 +/- 0.035 R\sun, where the temperatures of the primary components were adopted according to previous studies. The spectral type of the primary components was confirmed to be M4 and K5, and the mass ratio was derived as q = 0.920 +/- 0.003 or 0.952 +/- 0.010, respectively. We propose the presence of a third body in these systems: in the case of N103, a companion with a minimal mass of 50 MJup, orbiting the eclipsing pair with a period of about 19 years, and in 2M0410 a third body with a minimal mass of about 0.1 M\sun and a short orbital period of about 2.1 years. For N103, the hierarchical structure (2+1)+1 of a possible quadruple system was tested, but its stability was not proven. The characteristics and statistics of the flare events and dark regions on the surface of the components were estimated on the basis of the TESS and our own data. For N103, a mean frequency of flares of one per 40 hours was determined. In the case of 2M0410, practically no flares were detected.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes two low-mass eclipsing binaries (NSVS 01031772 and 2MASS J04100497+2931023) using VRIC and TESS light curves in PHOEBE. It improves absolute parameters, confirms spectral types (M4, K5), detects third-body companions (50 MJup with ~19 yr orbit for N103; ~0.1 M☉ with ~2.1 yr orbit for 2M0410), tests a (2+1)+1 quadruple hypothesis for N103, and reports flare statistics (one per 40 hours for N103; none for 2M0410).
AI.motivation: To study low-mass eclipsing binaries with short orbital periods and surface activity as part of a long-term observational project, improving absolute parameters and detecting potential hierarchical multiplicity.
AI.method: VRIC light curves were measured; TESS light curves were solved using PHOEBE in detached configuration; absolute parameters derived; third-body analyses performed via ETV or light travel time effects; flare and spot statistics estimated from TESS and ground-based data.
AI.result: Improved masses and radii for both binaries; spectral types M4 and K5; mass ratios q=0.920 and 0.952; third bodies detected (50 MJup, ~19 yr for N103; 0.1 M☉, ~2.1 yr for 2M0410); N103 flares ~1 per 40 hr; 2M0410 shows no flares; (2+1)+1 quadruple tested for N103 but unstable.
AI.conclusion: Both systems are detached binaries with low-mass components; presence of tertiary companions is proposed; hierarchical quadruple configuration for N103 is not stable; flare activity differs significantly between the two systems, suggesting different levels of magnetic activity.

title: Investigating white-light flare mechanisms via the Paschen jump using high-resolution continuum observations from the Swedish 1-m Solar Telescope
summary: The continuum is understood to contain a large portion of the energy emitted by a solar flare. The optical continuum, known as white light (WL), is particularly relevant since it may be observed by ground-based instruments. We measured the WL enhancements short- and longward of the Paschen jump in order to gain insights into the possible mechanism(s) behind the creation of these increases in our two case studies. We took measurements from the Swedish 1-m Solar Telescope of the pseudo-continuum around the Ca II 8542 Å line as well as the true continuum around the K I 7699 Å and the Fe I 6173 Å line, providing us with observations on both sides of the Paschen jump. We observe WL enhancements of over 40% against the dark (pen-) umbral background in both flares. The WL excess in flare 1 is co-temporal with the derivative of the GOES soft X-ray and hard X-ray (HXR) measurements from the Advanced Space-based Solar Observatory (ASO-S), and the flare is compatible with the Neupert effect. For flare 2, a preceding smaller flare may be the cause of the temporal discrepancy. Signatures of chromospheric evaporation and condensation are found in the WL area for both flares. The ratio of intensities blueward and redward of the Paschen jump in flare 1 is below one for most WL pixels. This is in disagreement with the accepted WL formation mechanisms. We believe this is a consequence of the Ca II 8542 Å pseudo-continuum being affected by line wing opacity changes. The co-temporality of WL and HXR enhancements suggests that the WL emission enhancements in flare 1 (and parts of flare 2) are a result of direct electron precipitation. We conclude that more reliable continuum measurements free of any nearby line influence are necessary in order to obtain conclusive evidence for the formation mechanism(s) behind optical continuum enhancements from such analysis as presented in this work.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes white-light (WL) enhancements in two solar flares using Swedish 1-m Solar Telescope observations on both sides of the Paschen jump. WL enhancements >40% were observed. Flare 1 shows WL co-temporal with HXR and compatible with the Neupert effect, suggesting direct electron precipitation. However, the Paschen jump intensity ratio contradicts accepted WL formation mechanisms, likely due to Ca II 8542 Å line wing opacity effects. The authors call for more reliable continuum measurements free of line influences.
AI.motivation: To investigate the formation mechanisms of white-light continuum enhancements in solar flares by measuring WL on both sides of the Paschen jump, using high-resolution ground-based observations.
AI.method: Observations from the Swedish 1-m Solar Telescope of pseudo-continuum around Ca II 8542 Å and true continuum around K I 7699 Å and Fe I 6173 Å, covering both sides of the Paschen jump. Measured WL enhancements against dark umbral background, compared with GOES soft X-ray, ASO-S hard X-ray data, and analyzed intensity ratios across the Paschen jump.
AI.result: WL enhancements >40% observed in both flares. Flare 1 shows co-temporality with HXR and derivative of SXR, consistent with Neupert effect, indicating direct electron precipitation. The blueward/redward Paschen jump intensity ratio <1 for most pixels, contradicting accepted WL formation models, attributed to Ca II 8542 Å pseudo-continuum line wing opacity effects.
AI.conclusion: WL enhancements in flare 1 (and parts of flare 2) likely result from direct electron precipitation. However, reliable continuum measurements free of nearby line influences are needed to conclusively determine the formation mechanism(s) of optical continuum enhancements.

title: Refined reduction and standardisation of 53 years of UBV photometry at Hvar
summary: Emission-line stars classified as Be exhibit light and colour variability on various timescales, ranging from days to decades. Their evolution must be constrained by long-term observations that are accurately calibrated and stable. Here, we provide a new reduction of photoelectric UBV observations obtained at the Hvar observatory, spanning more than 50 years. This unique dataset is highly complementary to TESS. We used new, non-linear reduction equations, with temporally variable extinction over the course of the night, which allowed us to achieve long-term accuracy of 0.008 to 0.016 mag (1-sig uncertainty), as verified by the Johnson standards. We then classified 59 Be stars into five classes, based on their variability patterns; namely, long-term envelope (LTE), long-term cyclic (LTC), binarity (BIN), rapid low-amplitude (RLA), and long-term quiescence (LTQ). According to our observations, the percentages of stars in the individual classes are 44%, 24%, 25%, 66%, and 19%, respectively. The stars in the sample often exhibited more than one pattern. We confirm that the LTE-positive variability is more common than the inverse (20 vs 6); in addition, two stars exhibited both types (zeta Tau and V1294 Aql). According to our observations, the LTC variability and the LTE-positive variability are almost mutually exclusive. Among 26 binary systems, circular orbits are more common than eccentric ones (18 vs 8). As for the brightness variations between different quiescent phases, an increasing trend is less common than a decreasing one (4 vs 7), spanning from -6.5 to +6.0 mmag/yr. Our observations provide well-calibrated UBV light curves spanning several decades, offering a valuable dataset for investigations of Be-star variability and tests of various models, including the viscous decretion disc model. Continuous monitoring is important for beta Lyr, EW Lac, delta Sco, gamma Cas, and V1294 Aql.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a new reduction of ~50 years of UBV photoelectric observations of 59 Be stars from Hvar Observatory, achieving 0.008–0.016 mag accuracy. The stars are classified into five variability classes (LTE, LTC, BIN, RLA, LTQ), with percentages of 44%, 24%, 25%, 66%, and 19% respectively. Key findings include: LTE-positive variability being more common than inverse (20 vs 6), LTC and LTE-positive variability being nearly mutually exclusive, circular orbits dominating over eccentric ones in binaries (18 vs 8), and decreasing brightness trends being more common than increasing ones between quiescent phases.
AI.motivation: Be stars exhibit complex light and colour variability across timescales from days to decades, and constraining their evolution requires long-term, accurately calibrated, and stable observations. The existing dataset from Hvar Observatory spanning over 50 years is highly complementary to TESS and needed improved reduction to enable reliable variability classification.
AI.method: New non-linear reduction equations incorporating temporally variable extinction over the course of the night were applied to photoelectric UBV observations. Stars were classified into five variability classes (LTE, LTC, BIN, RLA, LTQ) based on observed patterns in the light curves.
AI.result: Long-term accuracy of 0.008–0.016 mag achieved. Classification percentages: LTE 44%, LTC 24%, BIN 25%, RLA 66%, LTQ 19%. LTE-positive variability more common than inverse (20 vs 6). LTC and LTE-positive nearly mutually exclusive. Circular binaries outnumber eccentric ones (18 vs 8). Decreasing brightness trends between quiescent phases more common than increasing (7 vs 4), spanning -6.5 to +6.0 mmag/yr.
AI.conclusion: The well-calibrated UBV light curves spanning decades provide a valuable dataset for investigating Be-star variability and testing models, including the viscous decretion disc model. Continuous monitoring is particularly important for beta Lyr, EW Lac, delta Sco, gamma Cas, and V1294 Aql.

title: The Best Guess: Testing new and old formalisms for the common envelope against observations
comment: 29 pages, 14 (8+6) figures. Submitted to ApJ, all comments welcome
summary: We present a systematic test of formalisms for common envelope evolution by forward-modelling observable post-common envelope binaries. We compare predictions from the $α$-formalism, and the Two-stage and SCATTER formalisms against observed post-common envelope binaries, including wide binaries with ultra-massive white dwarfs and central binaries of planetary nebulae. The angular momentum-based SCATTER formalism does not predict populations which match the complete observed population, even with adjustments to its parameters. We take this as indicative of fundamental challenges with using the orbital angular momentum balance to predict common envelope outcomes. The energy-based $α$ and hybrid Two-stage formalisms both well-replicate the observed population. $α_{\rm CE} \sim 0.2\text{--}0.3$ can match current observations, in agreement with previous works. Recombination energy is necessary, but only a fraction of it ($\sim\! 10\text{--}40\%$) can contribute in order to predict IK Peg-like binaries with ultra-massive white dwarfs at the correct orbital periods. Our work suggests energy-based formalisms remain the most accurate for predicting common envelope outcomes, but more observations can constrain the recombination contribution and how these outcomes systematically vary with the donor mass.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically tests common envelope evolution formalisms (α, Two-stage, SCATTER) by forward-modelling observable post-common envelope binaries. Energy-based formalisms (α and Two-stage) best replicate observations, with α_CE ~ 0.2–0.3 and limited recombination energy (10–40%) needed for ultra-massive WD binaries like IK Peg. The angular-momentum-based SCATTER formalism fails to match observed populations, indicating fundamental issues with angular momentum balance approaches.
AI.motivation: To systematically test and compare different common envelope evolution formalisms by forward-modelling observed post-common envelope binaries, including wide binaries with ultra-massive white dwarfs and central binaries of planetary nebulae.
AI.method: Forward-modelling observable post-common envelope binaries and comparing predictions from the α-formalism, Two-stage formalism, and SCATTER formalism against observed populations.
AI.result: The SCATTER formalism fails to match observations even with parameter adjustments. Energy-based α and Two-stage formalisms successfully replicate the observed population. α_CE ~ 0.2–0.3 matches observations. Recombination energy is necessary but only 10–40% can contribute to predict IK Peg-like binaries with ultra-massive white dwarfs at correct orbital periods.
AI.conclusion: Energy-based formalisms remain the most accurate for predicting common envelope outcomes, while angular-momentum-based approaches face fundamental challenges. More observations are needed to constrain recombination contribution and systematic variations with donor mass.

title: Spectral Ratio Analysis: probing of a new suite of stellar activity indicators as a tool for astrophysical noise mitigation
summary: Stellar activity is the main barrier to detecting and/or confirming low-mass/long-period (and Earth-analogue) planets using radial-velocity (RV) measurements. Searching for reliable indicators that better trace magnetic activity may be key for both distinguishing more clearly between stellar and planetary signals, and for probing the underlying physics occurring on the stellar surface. In this work we have compared observations taken for magnetically active and inactive stellar phases over multiple time scales to study the spectral imprint due to varying stellar activity. This serves as a proof-of-concept demonstration of a technique (named Spectral Ratio Analysis, SRA) that can be used to isolate activity-driven changes directly in the stellar photospheric absorption lines where RVs are measured. Using 14 relatively quiet and well sampled G- and K-type stars that show stellar activity cycles, we identified hundreds of activity-sensitive spectral features. Reducing this variability information into two global metrics -- amplitude and velocity shift -- uncovers potential evidence of a decoupling of the photospheric and chromospheric responses to stellar activity in earlier-type stars. Additionally, potential signatures of the variations in the magnitude of the suppression of the convective blueshift throughout the activity cycle were observed via SRA. Finally, we show that these SRA indicators better capture RV variability than classical activity proxies, such as the chromospheric logR'HK index and other cross-correlation function-based parameters such as BIS and FWHM, by up to a factor of two. The direct link between photospheric line behaviour and stellar-induced RV variability offers a promising path for improving astrophysical noise mitigation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Spectral Ratio Analysis (SRA), a technique that isolates activity-driven changes in stellar photospheric absorption lines to improve radial-velocity planet detection. Using 14 G- and K-type stars, SRA identifies hundreds of activity-sensitive spectral features and shows that SRA indicators better capture RV variability than classical activity proxies by up to a factor of two.
AI.motivation: Stellar activity is the primary obstacle to detecting low-mass/long-period (and Earth-analogue) exoplanets using RV measurements. Existing activity proxies are insufficient for clearly distinguishing stellar signals from planetary signals.
AI.method: Spectral Ratio Analysis (SRA) compares observations taken during magnetically active and inactive stellar phases across multiple timescales to isolate activity-driven changes directly in stellar photospheric absorption lines. Applied to 14 quiet, well-sampled G- and K-type stars with activity cycles.
AI.result: Hundreds of activity-sensitive spectral features identified. Two global metrics (amplitude and velocity shift) reveal decoupling of photospheric and chromospheric responses in earlier-type stars, and signatures of convective blueshift suppression variations. SRA indicators capture RV variability up to 2× better than classical proxies (logR'HK, BIS, FWHM).
AI.conclusion: The direct link between photospheric line behavior and stellar-induced RV variability offers a promising path for improving astrophysical noise mitigation, potentially enabling detection of Earth-analogue planets.

title: White dwarfs within 13 pc: Insights from ultraviolet spectroscopy
comment: Submitted to MNRAS (2026-07-07). Open for comments and feedback
summary: We present a comprehensive multi-wavelength spectroscopic and photometric analysis of the 44 confirmed white dwarfs within 13 pc of the Sun. Combining flux-calibrated ultraviolet (UV) spectroscopy from the Hubble Space Telescope (STIS and COS) with ground-based optical spectroscopy, as well as photometry from Gaia, 2MASS, and WISE, we employ a hybrid fitting method to calculate atmospheric parameters. Each white dwarf was fitted with a bespoke model depending on its detailed atmospheric composition, aside from two strongly magnetic stars. We find a systematic discrepancy in H-atmosphere white dwarfs with Teff < 10,000 K, where fits incorporating UV spectra result in effective temperatures that are 2 - 6 per cent higher than those derived from optical and infrared photometry alone. We re-classify three He-rich white dwarfs as metal enriched following a magnesium detection in their near-UV spectra: WD 0435-088, WD 1132-325 and WD 1917+386. In total, we identify six stars in the sample for which metals were only detected in the UV. Overall we find that 30 per cent of the 13 pc white dwarfs show spectroscopic evidence of evolved planetary systems. Our analysis revealed no measurable difference between the hydrogen content of DQ and DC white dwarfs, although the upper limits of carbon in DCs are significantly below that of the DQ population. We find a multiplicity fraction of 32 per cent for the 13 pc white dwarfs.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive multi-wavelength analysis of 44 white dwarfs within 13 pc of the Sun reveals systematic temperature discrepancies in cool H-atmosphere white dwarfs when including UV data, re-classifies three He-rich white dwarfs as metal-enriched via UV magnesium detections, finds 30% show evidence of evolved planetary systems, and reports a 32% multiplicity fraction.
AI.motivation: To characterize the atmospheric properties, compositions, and multiplicity of the nearest white dwarf population using combined UV (HST) and optical spectroscopy plus multi-band photometry, enabling a more complete census of evolved planetary systems in the solar neighborhood.
AI.method: Hybrid fitting method applied to flux-calibrated UV spectroscopy (HST STIS/COS), ground-based optical spectroscopy, and photometry from Gaia, 2MASS, and WISE; bespoke atmospheric models tailored to each white dwarf's composition (excluding two strongly magnetic stars).
AI.result: (1) Systematic 2-6% higher Teff for H-atmosphere white dwarfs with Teff < 10,000 K when UV data are included. (2) Re-classification of three He-rich white dwarfs as metal-enriched via UV Mg detections. (3) Six stars with metals detected only in UV. (4) 30% show spectroscopic evidence of evolved planetary systems. (5) No measurable hydrogen content difference between DQ and DC white dwarfs, but carbon upper limits in DCs are significantly lower. (6) Multiplicity fraction of 32%.
AI.conclusion: UV data are critical for detecting metal pollution in white dwarfs and for accurate temperature determination; the local 13 pc sample shows a high incidence of evolved planetary systems and multiplicity, providing constraints on post-main-sequence planetary system evolution.
